summary: Gruber examines the growing hype around the App Store's sheer number of apps (approaching 100,000) and argues that quantity is being conflated with quality. He acknowledges that the large app count is a legitimate competitive advantage and marketing point for Apple, but warns against the Microsoft-style argument that a platform is great simply because it has the most software. He contends the iPhone is popular because it's great, not great because it's popular. He closes with the provocative hypothetical that if Android or WebOS delivered fewer but higher-quality apps, he would switch platforms, reinforcing that quality should remain the ultimate measure.
key_points: The App Store's size is a self-perpetuating competitive advantage: developers go where users are, and users go where software is
There's a critical distinction between 'the App Store is popular because the iPhone is great' and 'the iPhone is great because the App Store is popular' — conflating cause and effect is dangerous
Steve Ballmer's dismissal of iPhone apps as workarounds for the Internet reveals how deeply the iPhone disrupted Microsoft's worldview
Gruber would switch to a competing platform if it offered generally higher-quality apps, even with fewer total apps — quality over quantity remains his core value
money_quotes: The danger I see is in conflating cause and effect. Is the App Store popular because the iPhone is great? Or is the iPhone great because the App Store is popular?
The latter is the argument Microsoft has long made regarding the advantage of Windows: Windows is great because Windows has the most software and most developers.
The simplest reduction of the age-old Mac-vs.-Windows debate is quality-vs.-quantity.
You can't quantify the advantage Mac software has over Windows.
But my interest remains, as ever, in the quality of the apps, not the quantity.
That would make Android the Mac to the iPhone's Windows. I would switch to that platform.
Windows is proof that popularity doesn't guarantee market-leading quality.
key_insight: The sheer number of App Store apps is a powerful but potentially misleading competitive metric — popularity is self-reinforcing but doesn't guarantee quality, and conflating the two repeats Microsoft's old mistake in reverse.
